metadata.title: Why 'Good' in a Rubric Teaches Students Nothing About Quality
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut for claude-for-education-a-practitioners-guide candidate 06. Source chapter: claude-for-education-a-practitioners-guide/chapters/07-rubrics-exemplars-and-criteria.md (adjective-chain problem and observable-criteria mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper on newsprint; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, TEAL = observable criteria / actionable, CRIMSON = quality adjectives / vague

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = observable criteria / what you can see in the work; CRIMSON = quality adjective / assumes the judgment; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full six-question audit enumeration; NO analytic vs. single-point rubric comparison; NO AI exemplar disclosure controversy; NO rubric bias section. Core mechanism only: quality words describe quality with the word they were supposed to define — observable criteria describe what a reader would see.

beats.narration_text: A student receives a rubric mark: 'Analysis — Good. Demonstrates strong analytical thinking with minor areas for development.' She reads it. She learns nothing. She writes the next essay with no better idea of what analysis means in this course.
beats.card_text:
Analysis: Good.
Demonstrates strong analytical thinking
with minor areas for development.
beats.notes: CARD: the rubric cell in full. CRIMSON highlight on 'strong' and 'Good'. Narration continues past it.

beats.narration_text: A rubric is supposed to solve the transparency problem — students should know in advance what quality looks like. But a rubric written in vague language just moves the opacity earlier.
beats.notes: STILL: university student at a desk, rubric printout in hand, looking puzzled, warm desk light, editorial desaturated style.

beats.narration_text: A more detailed rubric should help students improve. Here is the case where a four-level rubric with named criteria produced the same confusion as no rubric at all. Why?
beats.card_text:
A detailed rubric should reduce confusion.
Here it didn't. Why?
beats.notes: THE QUESTION beat — named on screen AND narration.

beats.narration_text: Here is the structural failure. The rubric uses a quality word to describe quality. 'Excellent analysis — demonstrates sophisticated analysis.' The word 'sophisticated' was supposed to define what excellent looks like. Instead it just names the verdict. A student reading this learns only where they landed, not what to change.
beats.notes: Two rows: top row shows 'EXCELLENT' label → CRIMSON box: 'Demonstrates sophisticated analysis'. Arrow from 'sophisticated' loops back to 'EXCELLENT'. Label: 'defines quality with quality'. Bottom row shows TEAL replacement emerging.

beats.narration_text: Observable criteria describe what you would see in the work. Not a verdict — a behavior. 'Each body paragraph connects at least one piece of evidence directly to the paragraph's claim. The explanation interprets the evidence — it does not restate it.' A student reading that can self-assess before submitting.
beats.notes: Side-by-side: LEFT column (CRIMSON border): 'ADJECTIVE CHAIN' — 'sophisticated / strong / excellent / good'. RIGHT column (TEAL border): 'OBSERVABLE CRITERIA' — multi-line description with concrete verbs: connects, interprets, identifies, explains. TEAL label: 'can self-assess before submitting'.

beats.narration_text: The instructor asks Claude to audit one criterion. Claude flags 'sophisticated' as a quality word and proposes: 'Exemplary — each body paragraph connects at least one piece of evidence to the paragraph's claim and to the essay's central argument. The explanation interprets the evidence — it does not restate it.' The instructor checks whether 'interprets' itself needs a glossary entry. It does. She adds one sentence. Students can now self-assess.
beats.notes: Animated morph: old CRIMSON rubric cell ('Demonstrates sophisticated analysis') rewrites line by line into the new TEAL observable description. Each old word fades; new observable sentence writes in.

beats.narration_text: Quality words describe quality with the very word they were supposed to define. Observable criteria describe what a reader would see in the work. Claude can flag the adjectives. The teacher decides what quality means.
beats.card_text:
Observable criteria describe
what a reader would see.
Not a verdict — a behavior.
beats.notes: Quote card — the distinction that changes the problem.

beats.narration_text: One diagnostic prompt: paste your rubric and ask Claude to identify any performance level descriptions that use quality words rather than observable behaviors — and to list what it would need to know about your assignment to write an observable alternative. Then you make the call.
beats.notes: Notepad card. Text writes in: the diagnostic prompt. TEAL bracket: 'the audit'. Serif instruction below: 'if it flags your whole rubric — you have the right rubric problem'.

beats.narration_text: Two rules. First: the criterion must be observable — a reviewer of the student work and a reader of the rubric should be able to agree on whether it's met, without extra interpretation from you. Second: Claude drafts, you decide. The rubric is a tool for making your judgment legible, not a replacement for it.
beats.notes: Two TEAL cards stacked: Card 1 — 'OBSERVABLE: reviewer + reader can agree without interpretation'. Card 2 — 'HUMAN GATE: Claude drafts / teacher decides'. Both appear in sequence.

beats.narration_text: The rubric that says 'good analysis' is describing quality with the word it was supposed to define. One audit prompt changes that. The teacher still decides what quality means in this course.
beats.card_text:
Describe what you'd see.
Not how you feel about it.
beats.notes: Recap endcard. AI IN EDUCATION topic kicker.
